chunk_summary: Guidance on meal frequency, nutrient density, and feeding practices for children aged 6 months to adolescence, emphasizing age-appropriate meals, energy-dense foods, protein inclusion, and structured routines to address chronic undernutrition and stunting.
learning_objectives: Understand age-specific meal frequency and nutrient density recommendations for children aged 6–23 months.
Learn strategies to enhance energy density and protein intake in children with low appetite.
Recognize the importance of structured meal routines and physical activity for school-age children and adolescents.
Identify foods and practices to avoid, such as nutrient-displacing beverages and choking hazards.
key_facts: Breastfed infants aged 6–8 months need 2–3 meals/day; 9–23 months need 3–4 meals/day with snacks as needed.
Non-breastfed children require more meals plus milk feeds to meet nutritional needs.
Protein should be included in all main meals and at least one snack for school-age children and adolescents.
Energy density can be increased by adding oil/ghee to khichdi or dal, nut paste to porridge, or curd/egg to meals.
Tea can displace nutrients, and biscuits provide empty calories; replace with milk and home-prepared snacks.
Choking-risk precautions are essential for young children when offering nuts or hard foods.
reference_quote: Breastfed infants typically need 2–3 meals per day at 6–8 months and 3–4 meals per day at 9–23 months, with additional nutritious snacks as needed; non-breastfed children require more meals plus milk feeds. (WHO guideline for complementary feeding of infants and young children 6–23 months of age, 2023)
document_key_topics: Meal frequency and nutrient density for children 6–23 months
Structured meal planning for school-age children and adolescents
Strategies to enhance energy and protein intake
